content:
I have noticed that a lot of abstractions return impl PinInit<Devres<SomeType>, Error>. This isn't always necessary, as there are places in which it is guaranteed to be dropped as soon as the device is unbound from the driver:

Example 1 (probe in a Bus device):

#[pin_data]
struct SamplePlatformDriver {
    #[pin]
    iomem: Devres<IoMem<0x20>>,
}

impl platform::Driver for SamplePlatformDriver {

    fn probe(
        pdev: &platform::Device<Core>,
        info: Option<&Self::IdInfo>,
    } -> impl PinInit<Self, Error> {
        try_pin_init!(Self {
            iomem <- {
                let request = pdev.io_request_by_index(0).ok_or(ENODEV)?;
                request.iomap_sized::<0x20>()
            }
        })
    }

}

Devres provides no additional guarantee here, as it is dropped anyway as soon as the driver is unbound.

Example 2 (Devres in Devres):
(not a very realistic example, but it fullfils its purpose)

#[pin_data]
struct Data {
    #[pin]
    iomem: Devres<IoMem<0x20>>,
}

impl Data {

    fn new(dev: &platform::Device<Bound>) -> impl PinInit<Devres<Self>, Error> + '_ {
        Devres::new(dev.as_ref(), try_pin_init!(Self {
            iomem <- {
                let request = pdev.io_request_by_index(0).ok_or(ENODEV)?;
                request.iomap_sized::<0x20>()
            }
        })
    }

}

A field with Devres in a struct thats already wrapped in a Devres provides no addition guarantee.

Proposed solution:

Add a new trait DevboundPinInit:

pub unsafe trait DevboundPinInit<T: ?Sized, E = Infallible>: Sized {

    // the implementor needs to validate, that `dev` is the same device as they got passed as parent.
    unsafe fn __pinned_devbound_init(self, dev: &device::Device<Bound>, slot: *mut T) -> Result<(), E>;

    unsafe fn into_pin_init(self, dev: &device::Device<Bound>) -> impl PinInit<Self, Error> + '_ {
        // return a PinInit wrapper that calls self.__pinned_devbound_init
    }

}

unsafe impl<P: PinInit<T, E>, T: ?Sized, E> DevboundPinInit<T, E> for P {

    unsafe fn __pinned_devbound_init(self, _dev: &device::Device<Bound>, slot: *mut T) -> Result<(), E> {
        unsafe { self.__pinned_init(slot) }
    }

    unsafe fn into_pin_init(self, dev: &device::Device<Bound>) -> impl PinInit<Self, Error> + '_ {
        self
    }

}

Change the return value of probe in every bus device from PinInit to DevboundPinInit (as it guarantees to be dropped as soon the driver is unbound).

Change the parameter type in Devres::new from PinInit to DevboundPinInit to still allow Devres to be used, if we want to put in a Box or Arc for example.

Add a new macro try_devbound_pin_init that takes care of implementing the trait:

try_devbound_pin_init!(
    dev,
    < same syntax as try_pin_init! >
)

Example 1 with this solution:

#[pin_data]
struct SamplePlatformDriver {
    #[pin]
    iomem: IoMem<0x20>,
}

impl platform::Driver for SamplePlatformDriver {

    fn probe(
        pdev: &platform::Device<Core>,
        info: Option<&Self::IdInfo>,
    } -> impl DevboundPinInit<Self, Error> {
        try_devbound_pin_init!(pdev.as_ref(), Self {
            iomem <- {
                let request = pdev.io_request_by_index(0).ok_or(ENODEV)?;
                request.iomap_sized::<0x20>()
            }
        })
    }

}

Example 2 with this solution:

#[pin_data]
struct Data {
    #[pin]
    iomem: IoMem<0x20>,
}

impl Data {

    // This can still be wrapped into a Devres
    fn new(dev: &platform::Device<Bound>) -> impl DevboundPinInit<Self, Error> + '_ {
        try_devbound_pin_init!(dev.as_ref(), Self {
            iomem <- {
                let request = pdev.io_request_by_index(0).ok_or(ENODEV)?;
                request.iomap_sized::<0x20>()
            }
        }
    }

}

Advantages

  • more clean driver code
  • no unnecessary devm actions (this shouldn't be an issue).

Disadvantages

  • Adds a lot of complexity to pin-init.

I would like to hear your opinion and thoughts on the topic.

I don't understand the problem statement. Also, impl PinInit<T> has nothing to do with when the object will be dropped, so I don't get how the first example code using your solution wolud compile (since request.iomap_sized will return a devres.

@Benno Lossin

I don't understand the problem statement.

Devres is used in places where it technically wouldn't be required. This new trait would give the guarantee in the rust type system that the initialized object won't live longer than the device is bound to the driver. I am using a real driver this time as an example to make it more clear:

drivers/pwm/pwm_th1520.rs: (https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/ukleinek/linux.git/log/?h=pwm/for-next)
Th1520PwmDriverData has the field iomem: Devres<IoMem<TH1520_PWM_REG_SIZE>>. The Devres is not required here, as Th1520PwmDriverData gets dropped after pwm::Chip gets dropped/unregistered. This happens shortly before the device is unbound from the driver.
Note that IoMem<TH1520_PWM_REG_SIZE> never lifes longer in the driver than the device is bound to the driver, regardless of the use of Devres.
The trait would allow IoMem<TH1520_PWM_REG_SIZE> to be directly used as a field, removing all the

let iomem_accessor = data.iomem.access(parent_dev)?;
let iomap = iomem_accessor.deref();

lines from the code and instead use data.iomem directly.

Also, impl PinInit<T> has nothing to do with when the object will be dropped

Yes, thats why there should be a second trait which adds a gurantee that the object doesn't live longer than the device is bound to the driver.

so I don't get how the first example code using your solution would compile (since request.iomap_sized will return a devres.

request.iomap_sized would return impl DevboundPinInit<IoMem> instead after the change.
So the user has 2 choices:

  • let it initialize on another DevboundPinInit (the initializer guarantees that it won't live longer than device is bound to the driver).
  • use Devres::new to use it on a normal PinInit

Markus Probst said:

Devres is used in places where it technically wouldn't be required. This new trait would give the guarantee in the rust type system that the initialized object won't live longer than the device is bound to the driver.

how does your proposal address this? I don't see any type level constructs that would do that. You could have safety requirements that do that, but you haven't specified those.

I'd be curious to hear what @Danilo Krummrich has to say about this. I think it's a good idea to get rid of those access calls when possible, but this solution might require too many hoops to jump through.

Benno Lossin said:

Markus Probst said:

Devres is used in places where it technically wouldn't be required. This new trait would give the guarantee in the rust type system that the initialized object won't live longer than the device is bound to the driver.

how does your proposal address this? I don't see any type level constructs that would do that. You could have safety requirements that do that, but you haven't specified those.

Thats why DevboundPinInit::into_pin_data is unsafe. The caller guarantees that the initialized object will be dropped before the device will be unbound.

Then it can be initialized in the 2 places in which these guarantees exist:

  • Devres::new - into_pin_data will be called before passing it into Revocable::new
  • Device<CoreInternal>::set_drvdata - into_pin_data will be called before passing it into KBox::pin_init

(it can also be initialized on another DevboundPinInit with the try_devbound_pin_init! macro, that should use __pinned_devbound_pin_init directly though).
so the only two ways to use a DevboundPinInit safely are Devres::new and try_devbound_pin_init!, right?
in that case, we could also just use a token marker that can be passed down the call stack
Yes

I think the token approach is simpler, so:

impl<T: Send> Devres<T> {
    pub fn new<'a, E>(
        dev: &'a Device<Bound>,
        data: for<'b> impl FnOnce(DevresToken<'b>) -> impl PinInit<T, E> + 'a,
    ) -> impl PinInit<Self, Error> + 'a
    where
        T: 'a,
        Error: From<E>;
}
and then your driver type can require DevresToken<'_> as a parameter to ensure it is created from within a devres

Alternative solution:

trait DevboundPinInit<'a, T, E> {
  fn pin_init(self, token: DevresToken<'_>) -> impl PinInit<T, E> + 'a;
}

impl<P: PinInit<T, E>, T, E> DevboundPinInit<T, E> for P {
  fn pin_init(self, _token: DevresToken<'_>) -> impl PinInit<T, E> + 'a {
    self
  }
}

impl<F: for<'b> FnOnce<DevtresToken<'b>> -> impl PinInit<T, E>, T, E> DevboundPinInit<T, E> for F {
  fn pin_init(self, token: DevresToken<'_>) -> impl PinInit<T, E> + 'a {
    self(token)
  }
}

Devres then:

impl<T: Send> Devres<T> {
    pub fn new<'a, E>(
        dev: &'a Device<Bound>,
        data: impl DevboundPinInit<T, E> + 'a,
    ) -> impl PinInit<Self, Error> + 'a
    where
        T: 'a,
        Error: From<E>;
}

Just to avoid |_token| if we don't need the token.
Also impl DevboundPinInit is shorter, as it needs to be repeated on every driver implementation (the return type of platform::Driver::probe for example).

This would also allow for

#[pin_data]
pub struct DevboundArc<T> {
  inner: Arc<T>,
}

impl<T> DevboundArc<T> {

  pub fn new(token: DevresToken<'_>, data: impl DevboundPinInit<T, E>) -> impl PinInit<Self, Error>;

  pub fn clone(&self, token: DevresToken<'_>) -> impl PinInit<Self, Error>;

}

Avoids Arc<Devres<T>> and the .access call, which is needed if we use the resource in multiple handlers.
an initializer of an Arc shouldn't return PinInit
and this will result in the Arc being inside of a Devres, is that really the thing that you want?

No. DevboundArc::new will return PinInit, because it isn't allowed to move. This type is only allowed to be stored on types, that will be dropped before the driver is unbound. That way, the type is dropped before the driver is unbound, because the reference count will hit 0.
(No Arc<Devres> necessary)
you might be able to construct cycles with this DevboundArc :thinking:
at least when we get proper enum support for pin-init
Can you give me a code example?

struct Cyclic<T> {
    this: Mutex<Option<DevboundArc<Self>>>,
}
impl Cyclic<T> {
    fn new(token: DevresToken<'_>) -> impl PinInit<DevboundArc<Self>> {
        DevboundArc::new(token, Mutex::new(pin_init!(Cyclic { this: None })))
    }

    fn make_cyclic(self: &DevboundArc<Self>, token: DevresToken<'_>) {
        let clone = self.clone(token);
        let mut this: MutexGuard<'_, _> = self.this.lock();
        let this: Pin<&mut Option<DevboundArc<Self>>> = this.as_pin_mut();
        Pin::set_init(this, pin_init!(enum Option::Some(clone)));
    }
}
The Pin::set_init is like https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/pin/struct.Pin.html#method.set but for pin-init
(in fact using pin_chain, the example could be done in a single function, but I'll leave it as two for now)
Ok yes, this would be an issue. I am not sure how to avoid it though.
this also is an issue with the normal Arc and our DevresToken from before
since having a DevresToken does not mean that you're not inside of an Arc
and that problem also exists for your initial solution

Sure?

The DevresToken has a lifetime (including your version). So, we just need to make it, that the T in PinInit<T> relies on the lifetime of that token.
Edit: Might not work.

Side note: I am certain that my initial solution doesn't have that issue.

fn evil(token: DevresToke<'_>) -> impl PinInit<Struct> {
    PinInit::pin_chain(Struct {
        arc: Arc::pin_init(DriverData::new(token)),
    }, |finished| {
        let other = finished.arc.clone();
        // now give other to another thread etc.
    })
}

Markus Probst said:

Side note: I am certain that my initial solution doesn't have that issue.

yeah, I probably jumped the gun there a bit. the composing structure of the try_devbound_pin_init! helps here. But I'm not 100% sure it's sound yet

#[pin_data]
struct SamplePlatformDriver<'a> {
    #[pin]
    iomem: IoMem<'a, 0x20>,
}

impl<'a> platform::Driver for SamplePlatformDriver<'a> {

    fn probe(
        pdev: &platform::Device<Core>,
        info: Option<&Self::IdInfo>,
    } -> impl DevboundPinInit<'a, Self, Error> {
        |token: DevresToken<'a>| try_pin_init!(pdev.as_ref(), Self {
            iomem <- {
                let request = pdev.io_request_by_index(0).ok_or(ENODEV)?;
                request.iomap_sized::<0x20>(token)
            }
        })
    }

}

With the lifetime restriction, you can't send it to another thread or store it outside of the struct.
Creating an Arc, and storing the same arc inside the arc (creating a cycle), should still be possible though.

Having that lifetime is very bad from an ergonomic view, since that now infects the entirety of your program. Also what will that lifetime be filled in as, when you store a Devres<SamplePlatformDriver>?
'static. We just require that the function is able to accept every lifetime. At the end it would only be executed for 'static.

Having that lifetime is very bad from an ergonomic view, since that now infects the entirety of your program.

Then there would only be my initial solution left, which adds complexity to pin-init.
